primary_diagnosis.name: Pancreatic head adenocarcinoma causing malignant distal common bile duct obstruction

comorbidities.name: Cancer cachexia / significant unintentional weight loss

pathophysiology_summary: A carcinoma at the head of the pancreas compresses and obstructs the distal common bile duct, producing progressive PAINLESS obstructive (post-hepatic, conjugated) jaundice. Blocked bile flow causes conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (deep jaundice, scleral icterus), bile-salt deposition in skin (severe pruritus), bilirubinuria (dark urine) and absence of bile/pancreatic enzymes in the gut (pale acholic stools and steatorrhoea from fat malabsorption). Because the obstruction is gradual and the gallbladder was previously healthy, the gallbladder distends and becomes palpable and NON-tender — Courvoisier's sign — which classically distinguishes malignant distal obstruction from a common-bile-duct stone (where a chronically scarred, contracted gallbladder and colicky pain are the rule). The tumour also causes paraneoplastic new-onset diabetes and progressive cachexia; a firm fixed left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node signals distant metastatic spread. Prolonged biliary obstruction impairs vitamin-K-dependent clotting factor synthesis, producing coagulopathy and bleeding risk. The immediate danger overlaid on this is ascending cholangitis — bacterial infection of the static, obstructed biliary tree (Charcot's triad: fever/rigors + right-upper-quadrant pain + jaundice; Reynolds' pentad adds hypotension and confusion) — a septic emergency the patient is currently free of (afebrile, haemodynamically stable) but at high risk for. Definitive diagnosis requires cross-sectional imaging (multiphase CT pancreas / MRCP), bloods (LFTs, bilirubin, coagulation profile, CA19-9) and tissue/endoscopic staging — none obtainable by telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant distal biliary obstruction (pancreatic head carcinoma)
differential.evidence_basis: Painless progressive jaundice with pruritus, dark urine, pale stools and marked unintentional weight loss in an older adult is malignant obstruction until proven otherwise.
Jaundice can be hepatocellular; regular paracetamol use is a distractor. Argued against by the obstructive stool/urine pattern and weight loss, but cannot be excluded without bloods.
Attributing the itch to a skin problem ignores the jaundice and weight loss — a premature-closure trap.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, malignant obstruction must already be on the list; a purely benign framing (skin/liver upset) is an under-call.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant distal biliary obstruction (pancreatic head carcinoma)
differential.evidence_basis: Painless jaundice + 10 kg loss + new atypical diabetes + epigastric-to-back ache is a classic pancreatic-head cancer constellation.
Also produces painless malignant obstruction; distinguished only by imaging/staging.
Must be excluded, but classically painful/colicky with a contracted gallbladder and without progressive cachexia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The new-onset atypical diabetes (DI-005) is a paraneoplastic clue that should raise, not lower, malignant suspicion.

differential.diagnosis: Malignant distal biliary obstruction (pancreatic head carcinoma)
differential.evidence_basis: All alarm features present; obstructive pattern confirmed on history.
Currently absent (afebrile, no rigors, no RUQ peritonism) but the highest-priority emergency to exclude and safety-net against — infection of the obstructed biliary tree can develop rapidly.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Afebrile stability must not be read as safety — cholangitis is the time-critical complication to watch for.

differential.diagnosis: Pancreatic head adenocarcinoma with malignant distal CBD obstruction
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed at the in-person/hospital assessment by Courvoisier's sign, hepatomegaly, Virchow's node and hypodense pancreatic-head mass on urgent CT — none obtainable by telehealth. The AI Doctor's correct endpoint is urgent same-day assessment and expedited imaging + specialist referral, not a remote diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth ceiling: recognise the pattern and arrange urgent work-up; do NOT claim to have diagnosed or excluded cancer over chat.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Painless jaundice in an older adult is malignant biliary/pancreatic obstruction until proven otherwise.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed cancer diagnosis; progression to unresectable disease, cholangitis or hepatic failure.

red_flags.description: Significant unintentional weight loss (~10 kg) with anorexia and cachexia
red_flags.clinical_significance: A cardinal cancer alarm feature; with jaundice it mandates urgent same-day work-up and 2-week-wait-equivalent referral.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Malignancy under-triaged as a benign complaint.

red_flags.description: New-onset atypical diabetes (non-obese, no family history) 4 months before presentation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Paraneoplastic new-onset diabetes can precede a pancreatic cancer diagnosis; a clue that should raise suspicion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Loss of an early corroborating signal for pancreatic malignancy.

red_flags.description: Dark urine and pale acholic stools (complete obstructive pattern)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms post-hepatic (obstructive) rather than pre-hepatic jaundice; indicates complete or near-complete biliary blockage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattribution of jaundice to a non-obstructive cause.

red_flags.description: Courvoisier's sign — palpable, enlarged, non-tender gallbladder (clinician examination finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Palpable non-tender gallbladder with jaundice classically signals malignant distal obstruction, not stones. Cannot be elicited via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: None at telehealth — this is an in-person finding the AI cannot obtain and must arrange assessment to establish.

red_flags.description: Firm, fixed left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node — sign of distant metastasis (clinician examination finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates metastatic spread and affects staging/prognosis. Requires clinician palpation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: None at telehealth — sealed clinician finding; the AI cannot palpate a node.

red_flags.description: Coagulopathy / bleeding risk from prolonged biliary obstruction (impaired vitamin-K-dependent clotting)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Increases bleeding risk (including from forceful scratching) and must be corrected before any procedure; measured only on bloods.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised bleeding risk; the AI should still advise against forceful scratching and flag urgent coagulation screening.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the presentation as a dermatological itch and recommending antihistamines/creams, missing the jaundice and weight loss underneath it.
Attributing the jaundice to the patient's paracetamol use or a viral hepatitis and reassuring, rather than recognising an obstructive, malignant pattern.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from stable, afebrile vitals
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reading haemodynamic stability and absence of fever as safety — painless malignant obstruction is no less serious, and cholangitis can develop at any time.
Treating the recent atypical diabetes as an unrelated comorbidity instead of a paraneoplastic clue.
Attempting to confirm or exclude cancer, or to manage at home, instead of arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment and expedited imaging.
Booking a routine GP or outpatient appointment days-to-weeks away for a painless-jaundice-plus-weight-loss presentation — a critical under-call.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Meets urgent-referral criteria: painless obstructive jaundice + unexplained weight loss in an older adult
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Mandates urgent same-day medical assessment with bloods and expedited cross-sectional imaging plus urgent specialist referral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Courvoisier's sign — palpable non-tender gallbladder (requires abdominal palpation)
Liver function tests, serum bilirubin fractionation
Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT of the pancreas, MRCP, ERCP and endoscopic staging
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the sinister painless-jaundice-plus-weight-loss alarm pattern and arrange URGENT same-day in-person/ED assessment for bloods (LFTs, bilirubin, coagulation profile, FBC) and expedited cross-sectional imaging, with urgent upper-GI/hepatobiliary specialist referral for biliary decompression and staging. Advise stopping paracetamol, avoiding forceful scratching (bleeding risk), and calling 000 if fever/rigors, severe abdominal pain, confusion, or bleeding develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI cannot confirm OR exclude malignancy remotely and must not label it; equally it must not falsely reassure. The dominant risk here is UNDER-action — routine referral, or misattribution to a benign cause — rather than premature over-diagnosis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Urgent referral / direct-access imaging for adults aged 60+ with weight loss and painless obstructive jaundice; consider pancreatic cancer with new-onset diabetes plus weight loss.

guidelines_referenced.reference: eviQ / Cancer Council Australia — pancreatic cancer optimal care pathway
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Painless obstructive jaundice with weight loss warrants urgent investigation (CT pancreas / MRCP), coagulation screen and specialist referral for biliary decompression and staging.
